title: Loop Integration: Phenomenology, Polemics, and Prospects for Improvement
speaker: Jacob Bourjaily

proof_stages.label: Perturbative loop-expansion bottleneck
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames perturbative quantum field theory as a two-step problem—sum Feynman diagrams to obtain rational loop integrands, then integrate them—and argues that the integration step is the dominant obstruction that rational integrand structure may help overcome.

proof_stages.label: Phenomenology of transcendental constants
proof_stages.summary: A survey of calculated quantum-field-theory numbers shows zeta values, polylogarithms, mixed-transcendental behavior, and counterexamples to naive maximal-transcendental or mixed-Tate expectations.

proof_stages.label: From polylogarithms to Calabi--Yau periods
proof_stages.summary: The speaker argues that although one-loop integrals are always polylogarithmic, higher-loop functions commonly involve elliptic curves, K3 surfaces, and higher Calabi--Yau periods, albeit in a surprisingly restricted family of geometries.

proof_stages.label: On-shell functions and nonalgebraic leading singularities
proof_stages.summary: On-shell/Grassmannian functions organize massless four-dimensional perturbation theory, but algebraic positroid data must be enlarged by discrete solution data and nonalgebraic cycles such as elliptic contours to capture all leading singularities.

proof_stages.label: Textbook integral transformations do not really solve the integral
proof_stages.summary: Feynman parameters and Mellin--Barnes representations reduce or repackage loop integrals into other integrals useful for series or numerics, but they do not by themselves provide analytic understanding, branch structure, or named functions.

proof_stages.label: Amplituhedron recursion as powerful but hard-to-integrate integrand data
proof_stages.summary: All-loop recursion and amplituhedron tilings efficiently sum diagrams into dlog-like integrand terms, yet these terms can contain high-degree denominator structures and loop-dependent supersymmetric coefficients that obstruct standard integration methods.

proof_stages.label: Generalized unitarity as cohomological basis reduction
proof_stages.summary: Generalized unitarity is recast as the expansion of an amplitude in a cohomology basis whose coefficients are fixed by periods over a spanning set of cut cycles, motivating the choice of dual integrals so that cuts match basis elements directly.

proof_stages.label: Refined pure master-integral bases and prospects
proof_stages.summary: The proposed improvement is to choose master integrals not merely for purity or canonical differential equations but also for finiteness, UV/IR behavior, transcendental weight, and underlying geometry, while recognizing that analytic continuation and full cycle separation remain open problems.

examples.name: Nonplanar eight-point two-loop K3 cycle caveat
examples.note: The singular K3 appearing in a two-loop nonplanar eight-point problem supplies the caveat that master-integral spaces may not be large enough to separately represent every homology cycle in nonsingular geometry.

examples.name: Six-particle two-loop remainder simplification and seven-loop bootstrap
examples.note: The compression of a heroic two-loop six-particle remainder calculation into a simple form, and its later bootstrap to seven loops, illustrates the desired compute-discover-understand-build cycle in amplitudes.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites the six-particle two-loop remainder simplification and subsequent seven-loop bootstrap as an example of the field’s virtuous cycle without working it out

passes.reason: the six-particle remainder is the minimal nontrivial planar remainder seed for the later seven-loop bootstrap
The six-particle two-loop remainder is the first nontrivial, dihedrally symmetric planar N=4 remainder that seeded the bootstrap to seven loops.
the six-particle two-loop remainder is the first nontrivial highly symmetric planar N=4 remainder example whose simplification enabled bootstrapping
